16.1.label: Question 16.1
16.1.scenario:
A medium‐sized health insurance company has been issuing regular‐premium LTCI contracts for
   the past three years. The contracts provide different levels of benefit for different categories of
long‐term care. The policies are without‐profits contracts, with premiums payable until age 75, or
to the first benefit payment date, if earlier. Premiums and benefits increase automatically in line
with a published national index of residential and nursing home care costs.

The company has sold somewhat fewer policies than planned over the period, although the
average premium size has turned out significantly larger than expected. A number of
policyholders are already receiving benefits under their policies.

You are calculating the company’s supervisory reserves for the business in force at the end of the
current financial year.

(i).question:
State, with brief reasons, the principal elements of the supervisory valuation basis for this
business.

Advances in medical science can influenc:           transition rates between states: for example improved treatments may allow people to
stay independent for longer, reducing claim inception rates and the rates at which existing
claimants transfer to higher benefit levels (reducing claim costs)
          mortality rates: where improvements in medical science can prolong life, this will increase
the period for which care is needed (increasing claim costs); this is particularly significant
          care costs: medical advancements can alter the costs of medical care (drugs, mobility

(ii).question:
List the items of your own data that you would need in order to perform the valuation,
including those needed to enable you to determine an appropriate basis.

Solution: The difficulty lies in deciding on the distinction between, say, one claim consisting of a number of
separate payments, or a number of claims each consisting of one payment.
The important thing here is therefore to establish a definition of a claim, which is used
consistently for estimating (and modelling) claim frequency and the claim amounts that arise
from each claim.
Generally, people who keep their policies in force longer are more likely to claim than others,
and/or have a greater expectation of claiming in the future. The main reason for this is that
claims for pre‐existing medical conditions are normally excluded (through medical underwriting or
a moratorium clause) but as the policy continues in force, claims for new medical conditions will
arise (which will be covered).
The duration also has implications for renewal rates, as renewal rates tend to increase the longer
the policy has been in force.
          distribution of claim amount – indemnity, often without ceiling
▬          distribution of claim amount is dependent on hospital capacity, medical
science progress, insurer / provider deals, inflation
PMI policies usually provide indemnity benefits, paying for the actual cost of treatment
incurred. So the actuary has to estimate both the expected size of the claims that will
occur, and also the variance of the claims (to ascertain appropriate margins for risk). A
probability distribution of claim size therefore needs to be assumed for this purpose.
Note that this can consist of several separate payments to cover a series of treatments.
Long‐term care insurance also sometimes has indemnity benefits, (or part indemnity,
where there would be a contractual ceiling on the amount paid) and so this might also be
an issue when modelling claim amounts for this product too.
A policy with family cover will provide medical treatment costs for all members of a family unit,
for which a single ‘family rate’ of premium might be charged, and which might involve a range of
possible numbers of children.
The problem is that the claim cost will vary according to the number of children in the family. For
a new line of business, estimates will have to be made about the average number of children per
family and the likely effect this will have on claim costs. For an existing product, the past data can
be used to estimate future experience; here, however, there can still be a problem if family sizes
Normally, good quality underwriting would be expected to improve the experience for the
insurer, and so produce a more optimistic basis. For example, underwriting a critical illness
contract should result in lower critical illness rates in the future experience than if the contracts
had not been underwritten.
Inflation indices: The closest index would be an index of long‐term care costs (eg a residential and nursing home
fees index) if one is available. Failing that, because long‐term care is a people industry, an index
of national earnings is likely to be most suitable. Some of the costs are more to do with prices
(eg food) so a national prices index might also be relevant to some extent. A mixture of the two,
probably weighted in favour of the earnings index, would therefore probably be used. Additional
inflationary factors, particular to long‐term care costs, must also be considered (as discussed
\uf0b7 an absence of insurance: There is very little pooled industry data for LTCI. This is all part of the same problem
identified at the start of this subsection – the data are very sparse.
It will be necessary to estimate:          distribution of claim frequency – difficulty in defining a discrete claim
▬         claim frequency parameters are dependent on duration from policy inception,
age, gender, time of year, occupation etc
Claims incidence experience data will need to be subdivided, and analysed, according to
factors such as age, gender, time of year, occupation etc. This will enable future incidence
rates to be modelled more accurately.
Some of the factors may also be used as rating factors – ie where the insurer charges
different premiums for the different risk groups. Age, gender (if permitted by legislation)
Question: Why is estimating claim frequency (ie claim incidence rates) a problem?
What is the relevance of the duration from policy inception for claim frequency for PMI policies?
Why are the individuals on risk not necessarily known for family covers, and why is this a
problem?
What effect would the quality of underwriting have on a pricing basis?
\uf0b7 the impact of sales tax: Other influences on claims distributions include the macroeconomy, style of government
Macroeconomy: The macroeconomy affects the affordability of PMI policies. People in financial difficulties are
more likely to rely on the State for their health and care needs.
Some PMI policies can experience higher claim costs near the onset of a downturn in the
economy – eg members of group schemes may be tempted to get treatment for medical
conditions while still covered by their group policy, which would be forfeited if they were to lose
Style of government: Government policies can significantly affect public take up of PMI, firstly by making the State
healthcare provision more or less attractive, and secondly through the provision (or otherwise) of
direct incentives to take out insurance, such as a favourable tax treatment on PMI premiums.
Level of market competition: The more competitive the market, the lower the premiums tend to be. This also affects the
benefits that are to be found under products from different insurance companies. The PMI
market tends to be competitive on both counts. This also leads to product differentiation – with
no two insurance companies providing exactly the same benefits so that it is harder to compare
them and hence enabling each to charge higher premiums.
\uf0b7 the existence of NCD mult: As was outlined earlier, under a no claims discount (NCD) system, policyholders who
achieve a certain number of consecutive years without claiming are charged lower
premiums. A claim (or several claims) will result in the NCD being dropped for the next
year.
There may be several NCD ‘states’ – eg higher discount rates applied for policyholders
who have longer durations free of claim. These are the ‘multi‐states’ referred to above.
In some ways, a NCD operates like an excess. By imposing a penalty on claim (loss of
discount following a claim), policyholders are discouraged from making small claims,
thereby improving claims experience.
         the need to incorporate the reclaim of initial costs over several renewals in the
pricing model
The insurance company will need to make assumptions (from past experience) for the
rate of renewal in each year following the first issue of a contract. The excess initial
expenses and commission (over the renewal expenses and commission) can then be
spread over the number of years for which the policy is expected to be in force, based on
these assumptions.
The same premium is then charged for new business and renewals, each covering part of
the excess initial expenses. The renewal assumptions would be conservative (low), to
reduce the risk that the insurer suffers from unexpectedly high lapse rates and is then
unable to recoup the initial expenses fully.
         the need to model the chain of occurrence through GP referral via specialist to
hospital, plus the delay to settlement.
PMI claims often consist of a whole series of separate payments (eg an operation
followed by a series of follow‐up physiotherapy sessions) all of which would incur
separate treatment costs and hence separate claim payments. This ‘chain’ of claim events
should be considered when estimating the total claim costs arising from a given claim
event. It will, of course, differ by the type of condition being treated.
The settlement of claims – ie deciding whether a claim is valid and how much should be
paid out – is usually achieved very quickly for PMI. The delay (ie from the date that
treatment is carried out to the date the claim is paid) should be allowed for in modelling,
but as significant delays are relatively rare for PMI (compared to other kinds of short‐term
business) it is therefore not a critical modelling issue.
\uf0b7 an absence of insurance: This is the absence of any substantial pooled industry claim (and other) data, in many
This means that insurance companies that are new to the market will find it hard to
estimate future claim experience reliably, so they will have a large parameter risk. It is
also harder to establish industry‐wide trends in experience.
As a result, greater margins will be needed in pricing and reserving assumptions.
Furthermore, insurers will also find it harder to identify when their own experience differs
widely from its competitors, leading to increased risks of under‐ (or over‐) charging.
          problems with family covers where details of individuals on risk may not be known
          problems with group arrangements similarly, where details of individuals (especially
spouses and children) can only be estimated.
\uf0b7 the quality of underwrit: Anti‐selection is one of the biggest risks in the management of health and care insurance, as
people who consider themselves to be of high risk are most likely to apply to take out policies.
Underwriting is the insurer’s chief tool in combating the problems from anti‐selection: identifying
the high risk lives from risk‐related information provided by each applicant and charging
appropriate premiums (or other variations in terms) that should mean that the insurer should (at
least) cover its expected claim costs.
The ‘role of genetics’ contributes to the problem. Genetic tests now exist (and/or are being
developed) that enable individuals to find out more about their possible pre‐dispositions to
certain conditions. There is much ethical and political debate about the use that can or should be
made of such personal information (way beyond the scope of this subject), but in some countries
(eg the UK), at least some of this information does not have to be supplied to insurers when
underwriting. Potentially, this opens the way for increased anti‐selection, though its extent
remains to be seen, as the claims experience resulting from this practice is still young.
You do not need to know any detail of the genetic‐testing issues for Subject SP1.
Income protection insurance: According to the inception / disabled life annuity approach, the cost of claims incepted in
each future policy year (the expected claims cashflow) is worked out from:
\uf0b4\uf07bdiscount factor\uf07d \uf0b4: Claim inception rates are broadly equal to a sickness inception rate multiplied by the
probability of sick policyholders surviving sick for the deferred period.
An alternative, more complex methodology involves multi‐state modelling, in which
policyholders are separately tracked through the various stages of ‘healthy’ and ‘claiming’:
Such a model could be very complex, with hundreds of sub‐cohorts, and in practice, the
available data may not permit this degree of sophistication.
Critical illness insurance: Critical illness incidence rates are of the form: ix  ixhd  i xs  ixc  i xo , where hd, s, c and o
indicate causes of claim: heart disease, stroke, cancers and other.
An accelerated critical illness claim incidence rate is: i x  1  k x  q x , where kx is the
proportion of deaths over  x , x  1 that are due to critical illness.
A stand\u2010alone critical illness clai: i x  probability of surviving survival period .
Long\u2010term care insurance: Multiple‐state or inception / annuity methods can be used to value the benefits.
Private medical insurance: The risk premium (by age, gender etc) can be calculated as: RPx , s   ik ACk , where ik is
the claim incidence rate for benefit or procedure class k, and ACk is the expected cost of the
claim of that benefit or procedure class.
Accident and sickness insurance: This is similar to IP but shorter term; there may be some experience rating.
Health cash plans: This is similar to PMI except that the cost of some benefit classes is defined by a fixed
(contractual) amount per treatment, or per day of care. For the latter, it will be necessary to
estimate expected number of days of treatment in order to calculate ACk .
Health and care modelling complexities: There are a number of specific considerations to be made in modelling, and setting
assumptions for, the various aspects of each type of health and care product. These include:
Distribution of claim frequency \u2013 i:          transition intensities, including first, second and subsequent inceptions, and
recoveries; these depend on economic outlook, government provision of welfare
         theoretically up to forty different distributions, plus future trends (CI insurance)
\uf0b7 inception rates based on:          claim frequency parameters that depend on duration in‐force, age, gender, time of
year, occupation etc; there may also be issues in defining a discrete claim (PMI).
Distribution of claim amount – it is necessary to estimate the distribution of indemnity claims
for some types of LTCI and for PMI. This will depend on the state of the economy, inflation
and capacity of providers of medical / care services.
Claims may also be affected by advances in medical science and earlier diagnosis (especially
Sales volumes – these are generally difficult to model (all products) and may be affected by
\uf0b7 the quality of underwrit:           the use of multi‐state models (health states for IP insurance and LTCI, and NCD states
          guaranteed and reviewable product alternatives (IP insurance and CI insurance)
          different approaches for different claim types, eg disease‐based and
Data limitations \u2013 there is likely:           insured lives data may lack credibility, while worldwide statistics may lack relevance
(IP insurance):           for all illness except possibly cancers and heart attacks (CI insurance)
\uf0b7 insurance statistics may:           details of individual risks may not be known for family covers and group
The practice questions start on the next: keep the chapter summaries together for revision purposes.

(iii).question: Explain how you would determine the principal assumptions listed in part (i).

Solution: The sickness and claim inception rates would be equal when there is no deferred period, so that
the benefit payments commence immediately as the policyholder falls sick.
‘Claim inception rates’ are derived from ‘sickness inception rates’ by multiplying the
probability of sickness inception by the probability of remaining sick throughout the
deferred period.
Double counting will lead to an overestimate of claim incidence rates, which has less serious
consequences than understating the rates: it can lead to uncompetitive premiums, though is less
likely to threaten solvency.
These formulae are applied to each critical illness definition separately and then combined
to provide the overall risk premium for critical illness cover.
So rather than simply summing up the i xd terms as in Section 0, each i xd would first be multiplied
by its corresponding survival probability and then the results combined.
This process can be used in any profit testing methodology, whether single-state or multi-
state.
Note: the derivation of the above formulae is not required of students taking Subject SP1.
The policy will only pay claim amounts to the extent that they exceed the excess specified under
the policy. So, if an excess of £150 applies, a policyholder who incurs medical costs of £800 will
only receive £650 from the insurance policy.
A no claims discount is a reduction made to the premium payable in recognition of a
policyholder’s good recent claim experience. So, for example, no (or sufficiently few) claims over
the past five years may qualify a policyholder for the maximum possible discount, with a sliding
discount for fewer years of ‘no’ claims. The full premium might be payable for anyone who has
made a significant claim over the past two years.
One way would be only to use data on policies with the same level of excess (possibly adjusted for
inflation) to derive the incidence rates and average claims costs in the risk premium calculation.
If the gender‐specific price would have been significantly cheaper say, for males than for females,
the average price would be cheap for females and expensive for males. This may lead to anti‐
selection – more females and less males taking out policies than expected, making the actual
average claim experience heavier than assumed in the pricing basis. This will be made worse if
competitors charge different premiums to the two genders.
How to determine the assumptions: It would be appropriate to start from last year’s basis, which is therefore the ‘expected’ basis in
any investigation of how actual experience differs from expected.                                  [½]
Morbidity: It would be necessary to look at the investigation of how actual experience of transition rates
differs from those expected.                                                                    [½]
Causes of adverse discrepancy should be investigated, for example, whether high claims are due
to poor claims underwriting.                                                                 [½]
Any external data available should also be looked at, such as:                                        [¼]
         industry data                                                                               [¼]
         population data                                                                             [¼]
         reinsurers’ data.                                                                           [¼]
Trends in the external experience that might lead you to change your assumptions about future
experience should be identified.                                                             [½]
Consideration should be given to whether your company’s underwriting approach has differed
from what was originally planned (at entry or at claim), …                                 [½]
… or whether it is likely to change in the future.                                                   [½]
Have there been any changes in class of life taking out long‐term care (through changes in buying
habits or distribution methods, for example)?                                                  [½]
You should also consider whether there have been (or are likely to be) changes to medical or care
practice that may affect claims experience.                                                    [½]
Equally, there may have there been (or are likely to be) economic or political changes that might
affect incidence rates or rates of benefit inflation.                                           [½]
Selective withdrawals should also be considered.                                                     [½]
Investment: The expected returns from the assets backing the contracts would be assessed.                        [½]
For fixed interest securities, the current redemption yield would be important.                          [½]
For real assets such as equities and property, the long‐term economic outlook would be taken
Nominal yield assumptions would need to be consistent with the inflation assumption, bearing in
The volatility and risks associated with the assets involved would be taken into account when
setting a prudent interest rate assumption.                                                   [½]
The assumed returns from future investment would be based on an extrapolation of current
trends in accordance with the economic view.                                                             [½]
You should consider whether the assets backing the contract are likely to change (eg greater
investment in equities following an increase in the company’s free assets).                              [½]
Alternatively, if a market‐consistent approach is used then all cashflows would be discounted at
the risk‐free rate regardless of the actual assets chosen.                                      [1]
Inflation of benefits and premiums: You should consider current inflation rates (from historical changes in the given index).                [½]
Recent medium to long‐term trends should be extrapolated, …                                              [½]
… bearing in mind any expected changes in the economic and/or political environment in the
Future demand for long‐term care homes could make the relevant inflation rate differ from other
inflation indices (such as prices or wages), ...                                             [½]
... though analysis of the latter might still be useful.                                                 [½]
Price and wage inflation records would be important for judging future expense inflation.                [½]
An indication of market future inflation expectations could be obtained by analysing the real
yields obtainable on any index‐linked securities, at different assumed levels of future inflation. [½]
Expenses: A comparison of actual expenses with those assumed hitherto in the valuation basis is essential.
Discrepancies must be investigated to identify the cause.                                                [½]
\uf0b7 greater initial or claims:          greater administration costs (particularly of claims handling)
         greater costs from monitoring the experience.                      [½ each, maximum 1]
A projection of future expenses must be made, allowing for inflation.                              [½]
The projected contribution of fixed overheads per policy would also depend on future expected
… though you would usually also ensure that the assumed expenses would cover those required
should the company close to new business in the future.                                   [½]
Allowing for prudence: Regulations may require that assumptions are sufficiently prudent, ...                             [½]
... eg a margin might be incorporated that is large enough to cover the majority of future adverse
scenarios that could be considered to be ‘reasonably likely to happen’.                         [½]

Syllabus objectives: 4.1         Describe the main features of a health and care insurance model.
4.2         Understand and apply the techniques used in pricing health and care insurance
(The items shown in bold are covered – or partly covered – in this chapter.)
0 Introduction: This chapter, along with the next three chapters, cover the pricing of health and care insurance
contracts. Pricing is one of the most important uses of actuarial models, so it is natural that this
forms a core section of the Subject SP1 course.
The approaches described in this chapter apply mainly to individual (ie non-group)
Recall that the two main pricing methods are the formula and cashflow approaches, and it is the
cashflow approach that is generally used in practice, at least for long‐term insurance contracts.
This approach is also used for many life insurance products, the main difference between pricing
life and health insurance products being the modelling of the benefit cashflows.
This chapter focuses on modelling such benefit cashflows, which will enable the insurer to come
up with a risk premium for each of our main product types.
Chapter 18 deals with the pricing of group risks and Chapter 19 considers how to create a final
premium (starting with the risk premiums determined in this chapter) and some further general
1.1 Inception / disabled life annuity: The inception / disabled life annuity approach provides a method of determining the expected
cost of benefits from claims incepting in a particular year.
The inception / disabled life annuity approach is a simplified model that considers two
\uf0b7 the disabled life annuit: It ignores recoveries (or alternatively makes no distinction between recoveries and death)
Claim inception rate: The claim inception rate, i xd , is the probability that a claim will become payable to an
individual in the year of age x to x  1 . The individual will have become sick d weeks or
months (or years) earlier and remained so to benefit commencement.
Note that claim inception rates are diff:           Sickness inception rates relate to when individuals fall sick. In an IP insurance context, this
corresponds to the point in time when the individual is first deemed unable to work.
          Claim inception rates relate to the point in time at which benefit payments commence. In
an IP insurance context, this corresponds to the end of the deferred period.
Question: Explain how double‐counting could cause problems for the accelerated CI contract.
Why should the insurer not be too concerned about double counting for stand‐alone CI rates?
Explain the terms ‘excess’ and ‘no claims discount (NCD) rating structure’ (without looking them
up in the glossary).
Suggest how you might allow for an excess in calculating the risk premium.
List the benefits that would be provided on a typical cash plan contract.
Disabled life annuity: The disabled life annuity is the present value at the date of claim inception of expected
claim payments to individuals disabled after the deferred period until policy expiry.
Allowance is made for any escalation of the claim amount, interest and the probabilities of
death and recovery between the end of the deferred period and expiry date.
Expected claims outgo: Within the cashflow program, the claims outgo in any period is taken as the lump sum value
of the benefit (annual amount  disabled life annuity) multiplied by the probability of
becoming eligible for claim (claim inception rate).
The final steps would be to allow for the probability of being alive and not currently claiming at
the point of making a claim and to discount the value of this claims outgo back to the date of
So, in summary, the expected present value of claims incepting in a particular year can be derived
as the product of the following five components:
(1)       the probability that the policyholder is eligible to claim in the year under consideration
(for example, policyholders who are already claiming and policyholders who have died
before that year would not be eligible to claim) – this is sometimes referred to as a
(2)       the claim inception rate corresponding to the year under consideration
(3)       the value of the annuity then payable for the duration of the claim
(4)       a discount factor – this is needed to discount the annuity value from the point of claim
Note that these components are not uniquely defined and that there are different types of
eligibility probability, claim inception rate and disability annuity.
Multi\u2010state modelling was introduce: The inception / disabled life annuity approach is a simplification of a more complex
methodology, multi-state modelling. With multi-state modelling, policyholders are
separately tracked through the various stages of ‘healthy’ and ‘claiming’:
Each subclass will have its own set of transition probabilities: sickness inception, lapse,
Depending on the sophistication of the model, probabilities may vary according to the
number of previous times that the cohort has been ill and all transition rates may be a
function of the duration within that stage.
The actuary needs to recognise that the available data may not permit this degree of
sophistication of the method in practice.
Recall from Chapter 11 that:           in theory, the model could be very complex, with hundreds of sub‐cohorts open at any
          in practice, the lack of detailed, credible data is likely to necessitate the combination of
various sub‐cohorts and/or a reduction in the number of transition intensities required.
2 Critical illness insurance: The approach for CI is much simpler than for IP, as the benefit is a lump sum rather than an
Suppose it is possible to calculate inci: i xd  CI incidence rate due to cause d
An overall CI incidence rate can then be: i x  i xhd  i xs  i xc  i xo
Overlaps in incidence of certain critical illnesses may be allowed for explicitly.
Overlaps arise where more than one allowable critical illness cause underlies the same individual
claim. Thus it is possible for a person to suffer both from cancer and from heart disease, for
example, but only one claim will be paid under the policy. This should be automatically taken care
of if the assumptions for the incidence rates have been derived by analysing claim experience
data, provided care is taken to attribute each observed claim to a single (ie main) cause only.
The situation is harder if population medical records are being used to estimate future claim
rates. The medical data may record disease incidence (by cause) without necessarily indicating
whether other diseases are present at the same time. Double counting is therefore possible.
2.2 Accelerated CI insurance: A relatively simple approximation for risk premium rates for an accelerated critical illness
policy (ie one where the benefit is paid out on the earlier of death or critical illness) can be
ix          is the critical illness incidence rate
kx          is the proportion of deaths due to critical illness
These relate to expected experience over year of age  x , x  1  for a policyholder currently aged
By general reasoning we can feel comfortable with this formula. The expression ‘ k x q x ’
reduces the critical illness incidence rate to allow for those deaths that were caused by a
critical illness and are already included within the mortality rate.
Equally, you might think of this the other way round: the expression 1  k x  qx reduces the
mortality rate to allow for those deaths that were caused by a critical illness and so are already
included within the critical illness incidence rate.
\uf03d i x \uf02b \uf0281 \uf02d kx \uf0: Double counting (ie overstating the proportion kx of deaths that are also critical illness claims)
would understate the cost of claims and could lead to inadequate premiums or reserves.
The expected claim cost is then found by multiplying the claim incidence rate by the critical illness
Similarly we should express the stand-al: i x  probability of surviving the survival period
A stand‐alone critical illness policy will only pay a claim to a policyholder who survives the defined
survival period. So, if ix is the incidence rate of critical illness, then the claim incidence rate can
be determined by multiplying ix by the probability that these illnesses lead to claims.

The stand‐alone CI incidence rate will be the same for all policy durations at age 50 (provided
there are no temporary initial selection effects on critical illness rates). This will be:

3 Long\u2010term care insurance: The approach to pricing LTCI is very similar to that of income protection in Section 1 – one
can choose between the inception / annuity methodology or multi-state modelling.
A long\u2010term care insurance policy h: (1)         £20 per day if requiring a certain level of care in order to continue living in own home
(2)         £50 per day if requiring residential (non‐nursing) home care
(3) \u00a3150 per day if requiri: A multi‐state model can be defined that describes how to calculate the expected present value of
the benefits for an initially healthy policyholder aged 70. Assume that it is not possible for
someone to move from a higher benefit level to a lower one.
Dead: In order to calculate the expected present value of the benefits for an initially healthy
policyholder aged 70, it would be necessary to assume forces of transition between states (one
transition rate for each arrow in the diagram above). These would be used to project the
probabilities of being in particular states at particular future times.
These probabilities can then be multiplied by the corresponding cashflow associated with being in
that state.
Appropriate dependencies may need to be allowed for, eg rates of transition to higher benefit
levels may need to vary by duration in current state and by cause of incapacity.
Alternatively, an inception / disabled life annuity approach can be used to value the policy
benefits for a new policyholder aged 70. All policyholders who claim under the policy can be
assumed to move between benefit levels in order of increasing payment (ie it is not possible to
start claiming at benefit level (2) without first receiving benefit at level (1), for example).
Claim inception at each future age 70  t would be needed. There would be three rates of claim
inception at each age, relating to claims starting at each benefit level.
It would probably be easiest to use central rates of claim inception for this purpose. It would then
be necessary to multiply by the probability of the policyholder surviving from 70 to the middle of
year [70  t ,70  t  1] to give the probability of claiming in that year (again, for each benefit level
separately).
The probability of claiming at each future age for each benefit level would then need to be
multiplied by the appropriate current‐claim disability annuity values, and by the projected
increase in benefit level at the time of commencement of (or increase in) claim.
So in total, three tables of these annuity values would be needed: one for each benefit level, and
each subdivided by age at claim inception. These would be whole life disability annuities, with
payments ceasing on death (assuming recovery is impossible).
The annuity functions would include appropriate allowance for future inflation of benefit
payments in each case. The projected mid‐year values would then need to be discounted to the
start of the policy.

4.1 Calculating a risk premium: The risk premium of a short‐term insurance policy is the element of the premium required to
cover the expected claim amount only. It includes no allowance for expenses, profit etc. These
As for many modelling exercises, the pro:          Choose a base period over which to collect claims and exposure data.
         Collect data, checking the accuracy and appropriateness of the data.
\uf0b7 Analyse the data, eg to i:          Adjust and project forward to obtain future risk premiums, ie the expected claim amount
over the period that policies covered by the premium rates will be in force.
Risk premiums will be calculated for each product and level of cover (eg hospital band).
Private medical insurance is priced on an incidence x average claim cost basis.
Risk premium (age, gender) \uf03d \uf0e5: where               i k is the incidence rate for benefit / procedure class k
and                 ACk is the average claim cost for benefit / procedure class k.
i k and ACk are specific to each age and gender and the summation is over all classes k.
Age bands (eg 5‐year or 10‐year bands) may be used rather than individual ages in order to
ensure there are sufficient data in each cell, or perhaps because the product is priced in these age
Depending on market practice, available information and risk aspects in particular
territories, the risk premium determinants may be extended beyond age and gender,
eg location, hospital band, smoker status, occupation.
What competitors do in this respect is often the most significant driving force towards having
However, in some territories there may be equality legislation that prevents or restricts
differential pricing, even by age and/or gender.
Multi-state modelling will be used if long-term guarantees are present.
If premiums are guaranteed for a period longer than one year, the approach to pricing may be
similar to that of a long‐term product.
4.2 Adjustments: The formula above will be adjusted for excesses, the presence of a no claims discount
(NCD) rating structure, if one exists, and any other product features that may impact the
incidence rate or average claim cost.
Alternatively, data for which no excess:           Reduce the incidence to allow only for the proportion of claims expected to exceed the
excess amount. It may be necessary to assume that claim amounts follow a particular

          Reduce the incidence further to allow for the fact that the excess acts as a disincentive to
claiming, especially if the excess is voluntary. Ideally this reduction would be based on
          Adjust the average claims cost to allow for the excess. The reduction won’t be the full
excess amount as some of the claims would have been for less than the excess. (Again this
could be deduced with the aid of an assumed claim amount distribution.)

Ultimately the gender-specific premiums may be combined in the office premium
This would be necessary if the insurer was required to charge the same premium rates to males
and females. To do this, it would be necessary to weight the gender‐specific rates in proportion
to the expected ratio of males to females amongst the people taking out policies.
5 Accident and sickness insurance: Short-term accident and sickness insurance is often provided to protect loan repayments.
As with income protection, the rating method often follows the inception / annuity approach.
However, an average rate is often agreed for a particular lender or scheme, and this brings
another layer of complexity to the rating formula.
Remember that accident and sickness policies will only provide regular payments for a few years
(usually less than five), but otherwise are very similar to the standard IP design.
The idea of an ‘average rate’ applicable to the lender or scheme is to charge the same premium
rate (ie per £ of the loan) to all policyholders, regardless of their age, gender etc.
Large schemes tend to be rated using the ‘group’ techniques described in the next chapter.
The ‘group techniques’ referred to include such things as experience rating (described in the next
6 Health cash plans: Premiums for health cash plans are determined by first calculating the expected claims for
each of the benefits – typically an inception rate multiplied by the average benefit expected
The expected benefit will take account of any excesses or coinsurance factors that apply.
Typically, each of the above benefits will have limits on the number of payments or total amount
payable in each year.
Some benefits will provide indemnity of costs up to a maximum annual limit. For example, the
cash plan may reimburse 50% of physiotherapy treatment costs up to a maximum of £200 in any
one year. In this case, the expected average cost of the benefit would be used in the premium
calculation, rather than the benefit amount. The use of coinsurance (with the policyholder) is
common in order to reduce anti‐selection and keep premiums at an affordable level.
Excesses or coinsurance (with the policyholder) can also apply to benefits that are fixed in amount
by the policy conditions. These can occur because, while the amounts of benefit may be fixed,
the total number of payments associated with a single claim event might not be. For example,
these plans often provide a fixed payment per day spent in hospital. So an excess might apply
such that the policyholder only receives benefits for (say) the fourth and subsequent consecutive
days of hospital stay.
For many of the benefits approximations are made, depending largely on the company’s
previous claim experience. Difficulties can arise in establishing a suitable age (range)
estimate on which to determine a flat rate.
This is the case where the same rates of premium are charged for these contracts over broad
ranges of ages, even though claim incidence may vary significantly by age for some benefits.
The total calculated is then adjusted to take account of ‘inertia’, ie people’s propensity not
to claim even when they are entitled to. This arises for two (related) reasons:
          The first is that individuals may buy the plan with a particular benefit in mind, and
tend to forget the rest of the cover provided by the policy.
          The second is that the size of the benefits in absolute terms tends to be small, and
this combined with ‘customer apathy’ leads to many otherwise valid payments not
being claimed.
Of course, if the ‘inertia’ is already in the claims experience data (eg the insurer’s own claims data
are used), no further adjustment would be required – unless the extent of the inertia has changed
or is expected to do so. The level of inertia might change if, for example, the product was
marketed differently or there was a change in the market making people more aware of cash plan
benefits.
7 Health and care insurance modellin: The Core Reading in this section gives a list of some of the key modelling issues that relate to each
of the main health and care insurance products. While these are mainly concerned with
modelling, some also relate to setting assumptions – in particular pricing assumptions, which is
why they appear here. There are lots of questions in this section to encourage you to read the
material actively. Some of the issues should be very familiar to you already, while others may be
Some of the issues to be considered in the modelling of the various aspects of health and
care insurance products are listed below.
The examination requires an awareness of the relevance of each point to the product being
modelled – but students are not expected to expand on these in detail.
It will be necessary to estimate:          transition intensities – possibly need to separate out second and subsequent
▬         this includes recoveries, eg by duration of claim or by type of incapacity
\uf0b7 sales volumes, which are: In order to use transition intensities in the modelling process, it is necessary to convert them into
transition probabilities first, before using them to construct projected numbers (or proportions) in
each state at future ages (assuming a multiple‐state methodology is being used).
Alternatively, the transition rates (and derived transition probabilities) can be used to calculate
claim inception rates and disability annuity values (if the inception and disability annuity method
It may be necessary to separate out second and subsequent sickness incidences because
individuals who have had periods off work through sickness in the past may be more likely to
claim again in the future, compared with those that have never claimed.
It is important to estimate recovery rates by duration of claim as recovery rates reduce as the
duration of the claim increases. In order to produce accurate estimates, it is therefore necessary
to use recovery rate parameters that vary according to claim duration.
Recovery rates also differ dramatically according to the cause of the sickness underlying the claim.
So, again, in order to produce accurate estimates of recovery rates it is necessary to have
separate recovery rates for the different major causes of incapacity.

Other influences on transition intensities (and sales volumes) include economic morale,
